serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bottle of drinking water, typically 500ml (approximately 500g).

nutrient_source.title: Water, tap, drinking
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central entry for plain drinking water (tap, drinking). Values represent the universally accepted nutritional profile of purified/bottled drinking water.

gi_source.title: Water — glycemic index reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (general scientific consensus)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is effectively zero as water contains no carbohydrates; set to 1 (lowest valid positive value) and gi_category 'low' per Veritas v2 convention for zero-GI beverages. Glycemic load per 500g serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0.

hypertension.guidance: Plain bottled water contains negligible sodium (approximately 1 mg per 100g) and is the preferred hydration choice for individuals with hypertension, as adequate daily water intake supports blood pressure regulation without contributing dietary sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Absence of carbohydrates and glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bottled drinking water contains zero carbohydrates and zero sugar, exerts no glycemic effect, and is the recommended primary beverage for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, replacing sugar-sweetened beverages to support glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Hydration, blood viscosity, and absence of atherogenic nutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Adequate hydration with plain water supports cardiovascular health by maintaining optimal blood viscosity, facilitating nutrient and oxygen transport, and avoiding the saturated fat, sodium, and added sugars present in less healthy beverage alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bottled drinking water is purified or filtered water packaged in sealed plastic or glass bottles for convenient, safe consumption. In Singapore and across Asia, bottled water is widely sold at restaurants, hawker centres, convenience stores, and food delivery platforms as a standard accompaniment to meals. The product sold at Chinese restaurants such as Wan Jia Fu is typically a commercially purified still water (纯净水, chúnjìngshuǐ), processed through reverse osmosis or multi-stage filtration to remove impurities, minerals, and microorganisms, resulting in a clean, neutral taste with no odour, colour, or flavour. Texturally, it is a clear, still liquid with no carbonation. Nutritionally, bottled drinking water contains zero calories, zero protein, zero fat, zero carbohydrates, and zero sugar, making it the most nutritionally neutral beverage available. Trace amounts of sodium, potassium, calcium, and iron may be present depending on the source water and filtration method, but these are nutritionally insignificant. From a clinical perspective, plain water is universally recommended for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, obesity, and kidney conditions, as it provides essential hydration without contributing to caloric intake, glycaemic load, sodium burden, or lipid profiles. Adequate daily water intake (approximately 2–2.5 litres for adults) supports metabolic function, thermoregulation, renal clearance, and cardiovascular efficiency. Bottled water is suitable for all dietary patterns including vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, and gluten-free diets.
common_user_queries: is purified water the same as mineral water

confidence_reason: Drinking water is a universally understood and nutritionally consistent item. Nutrient values are confirmed zero or trace per USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 173873). Multilingual names are standard verified translations. No estimation or analogy was required for nutrient values.
notes: Drinking water (bottled, purified); energy_kcal is naturally zero as water contains no caloric macronutrients. protein_g is naturally zero as water contains no protein. fat_g is naturally zero as water contains no fat. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as water contains no carbohydrates. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, sugar_g, dietary_fibre_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are all confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Trace sodium (1 mg), potassium (1 mg), calcium (1 mg), and iron (0.01 mg) per 100g reflect typical values for tap/purified drinking water per USDA FoodData Central. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per Veritas v2 convention for zero-GI beverages. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0. Served at Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant (万家福创新中国菜) on field survey data at SGD 2.89 per bottle.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected invalid JSON output and populated all mandatory fields.
Iteration 2: Applied zero-macro justification rule, zero-GI beverage convention, multilingual naming, ontology codes, and health context.

Iteration 3: Substantially expanded natural_language_description to cover preparation method (reverse osmosis, multi-stage filtration), sensory profile (neutral taste, no odour, no colour, still liquid), cultural context (Singapore restaurants, hawker centres, field survey delivery, Chinese restaurant context), clinical relevance across five conditions, and nutritional profile detail — resolving q6_llm_ready_semantics failure from iteration 2 validator feedback.
